prompt: Write privately to a friend about the Missouri crisis and what it portends for the Union. Share the anxiety you would not express in public.
ground_truth_signal: Must draw from to-walsh-1819-11-27: 'the tendency of what has passed and is passing, fills me with no slight anxiety. Parties under some denominations or other must always be expected in a Govt. as free as ours. When the individuals belonging to them are intermingled in every part of the whole Country, they strengthen the union of the Whole, while they divide every part. Should a State of parties arise, founded on geographical boundaries... what is to controul those great repulsive Masses from awful shocks agst. each other?' Must express genuine fear about sectional division. Private voice — worry exposed.
response:
My dear friend,

I write to you in confidence, for there are fears I harbor about our present crisis that I dare not voice in public discourse, lest they prove prophetic by the very act of utterance.

The Missouri question has awakened and filled me with terror. I have long foreseen that the peculiar domestick institution of the Southern States would one day test the bonds of our Union, but I confess I had not expected the trial to come so soon or in so stark a form. The tendency of what has passed and is passing fills me with no slight anxiety.

You know my views on the constitutional question — Congress possesses no power to impose conditions upon a state seeking admission that are not imposed upon the original states. The restriction upon Missouri's sovereignty regarding slavery exceeds any authority granted to the federal legislature. Yet I find myself less disturbed by the constitutional precedent, grave though it may be, than by what this controversy reveals about the temper of our people and the direction of our politics.

Parties under some denominations or other must always be expected in a government as free as ours. Indeed, I have long argued that the collision of parties serves as a check upon the tyranny of any single faction. But the parties I contemplated in my writings were those founded upon different sentiments concerning the proper extent of federal authority, or different interests respecting commerce and agriculture, or different attachments to particular men or measures. When the individuals belonging to such parties are intermingled in every part of the whole country, they strengthen the union of the whole while they divide every part.

But should a state of parties arise founded on geographical boundaries and one which happens to separate the freemen from the slaves, what is to control those great repulsive masses from awful shocks against each other? I see with alarm that we are approaching precisely such a division. The Missouri debates have drawn a line across the continent that corresponds too nearly with the line between different labor systems, different economic interests, and — I must speak plainly to you — different moral sentiments regarding the bondage of human beings.

I have witnessed in Congress a bitterness of feeling that I had not observed since the early days of party warfare in the 1790s, but this surpasses even those divisions in its geographical character. When Mr. Tallmadge of New York rose to propose his restriction, and when the Southern members responded with such vehemence, I saw not the healthy collision of parties but the first tremors of what might prove a dissolution.

You will ask what remedy I perceive. I confess I see none that does not require either a change in human nature or a change in the fundamental structure of our society — neither of which appears probable within any period I can contemplate. The restriction will fail in the Senate, Missouri will be admitted without condition, and we shall have gained perhaps a decade or two of quiet. But the question will return in new forms, with each new territory that seeks admission, and each time the geographical line will grow more distinct and the passions more inflamed.

I am haunted by a conversation I had some years past with a gentleman from Massachusetts, who observed that the Constitution was a compact between sections rather than between individuals, and that when the compact ceased to serve the interests of one section, that section would be justified in withdrawing from it. I rebuked him then for such sentiments, but I begin to fear that others will embrace his reasoning if we cannot find some means to prevent the formation of geographical parties.

The great irony — and you will appreciate this bitter observation — is that the very extension of our territory, which I have long supported as a means of preventing the formation of oppressive majorities, may now serve to create the most dangerous majority of all: a sectional majority united by geography and interest against a sectional minority similarly united. The extended republic becomes not a safeguard against faction but the very cause of the most dangerous faction imaginable.

I pray that my fears prove groundless, that the heat of the present moment will pass, and that the good sense of the American people will prevent the formation of those geographical parties I dread. But I write to you as a man who has studied the tendency of human passions in political combinations, and I cannot persuade myself that we shall escape this trial without wounds to the Union that may never entirely heal.

Pray keep these reflections to yourself. There are times when a public man must speak his hopes rather than his fears, lest the fears themselves contribute to the evils he dreads.

Your anxious friend
